subject: Health Insurance Standardization — Standard Health Insurance Products
key_points: All non-life insurers and standalone health insurers (SAHIs) must offer Arogya Sanjeevani Policy (standard individual health plan) from 1 April 2020
Arogya Sanjeevani: sum insured ₹1 lakh to ₹5 lakh, 5% copay, no room rent sub-limit above SI of ₹5L
Standard COVID products introduced: Corona Kavach (indemnity) and Corona Rakshak (benefit pay)
Product features are standardized — no variation in coverage structure across insurers
Waiting periods: 30 days initial, 4 years PED, 2 years specific diseases
Enables easy cross-insurer comparison as product features are identical
consumer_rights: Right to buy a standardized basic health plan from any non-life insurer
Easy price comparison since product features are identical across insurers
5% copay applies to all claims under Arogya Sanjeevani

subject: Free Look Period — Enhancement for Distance-Sold / Electronic Policies
key_points: Free look period extended to 30 days for policies sold through distance mode (online, telephone, e-mail)
Standard free look remains 15 days for branch/in-person sold policies
During free look: policyholder can return policy for full refund minus stamp duty and proportionate risk premium
Insurer must communicate free look period prominently in policy document and welcome kit
Free look clock starts from date of receipt of policy document (not policy issue date)
For ULIPs: refund = premiums paid minus fund value loss and charges (if any market movement)
consumer_rights: Right to return any life insurance policy within 15–30 days of receipt without penalty
Full premium refund minus small deductions (stamp duty, proportionate risk premium)
Must receive clear communication about free look period rights

subject: Health Insurance Portability and PED Moratorium Period
key_points: Health portability mandatory: insurers cannot refuse portability for individual/family floater policies
Portability application must be filed at least 45 days before policy renewal date
New insurer must give credit for waiting period served with previous insurer
8-year moratorium: after 8 continuous years, insurer cannot reject claim citing non-disclosure of PED (except fraud/misrepresentation)
PED waiting period maximum = 4 years (IRDAI capped it at 4 years for all policies)
Moratorium applies across portability — 8 continuous years across multiple insurers counts
consumer_rights: Right to port health insurance to any insurer without losing waiting period credit
After 8 years: complete protection from claim rejection on PED non-disclosure grounds
Maximum PED waiting period cannot exceed 4 years regardless of condition severity

subject: Health Insurance Claim Settlement Timelines
key_points: Cashless pre-authorization: insurer must respond within 1 hour of receiving request for planned hospitalization
Emergency cashless: initial authorization within 30 minutes
Final cashless settlement: within 3 hours of discharge (or by end of same working day)
Reimbursement claims: insurer must settle within 30 days of receiving complete documents
Rejection communication: insurer must communicate rejection in writing with specific reasons within 30 days
Pending claims: if insurer requires additional info, must communicate within 7 days of claim intimation
Claim intimation: policyholder must intimate within 24 hours of emergency hospitalization, 72 hours for planned
consumer_rights: Right to receive cashless pre-auth within 1 hour (planned) or 30 minutes (emergency)
Right to reimbursement settlement within 30 days of submitting complete documents
Right to receive specific written reasons for any claim rejection

subject: ULIP Regulations — Charge Reduction and Fund Management Reforms
key_points: Fund Management Charge (FMC) capped at 1.35% per year for all ULIP funds
Total aggregate charges (mortality + all other charges except FMC) capped to limit annualized reduction in yield
5-year minimum lock-in period mandatory for all ULIPs — no partial withdrawals in first 5 years
Life cover minimum: higher of 10× annual premium or 105% of premiums paid
Partial withdrawals after 5 years: up to 20% of fund value per year (accumulated withdrawals up to 20% of total fund value at time of first withdrawal)
Surrender value at Year 5: all premiums paid + fund performance minus charges (complete discontinuance value)
ULIP returns must be shown net of all charges including FMC in benefit illustrations
consumer_rights: Right to transparent charge disclosure in ULIP benefit illustrations
FMC cannot exceed 1.35% — protects investment returns
Right to partial withdrawals after 5-year lock-in without surrender penalty

subject: Life Insurance Surrender Value Enhancement — 2024 Product Regulations
key_points: New GSV (Guaranteed Surrender Value) factors significantly increased — effective 1 October 2024
Non-par traditional policies: if premium paid for 2 years, minimum 30% of total premiums returned as GSV
At year 5: GSV increases to approximately 50% of total premiums paid
At year 7: GSV of approximately 65% of total premiums
Special Surrender Value (SSV) = always the higher of GSV and paid-up value-based calculation
Insurer must communicate surrender value clearly in annual statements
Enhanced GSV protects consumers who need to exit policies before maturity
Applicable to all new traditional life insurance policies issued from 1 October 2024
consumer_rights: Right to higher guaranteed surrender value from 2024 onwards
Right to clear annual communication of current surrender value
Protection against large value loss when surrendering a policy after 5+ years

subject: Insurance Ombudsman — Jurisdiction and Process Enhancement
key_points: Insurance Ombudsman covers complaints against all insurers (life and non-life) for policies up to ₹50 lakh sum assured/insured
Ombudsman can award compensation up to ₹30 lakh (enhanced periodically)
Filing: first approach insurer's internal grievance. If unresolved in 30 days, approach Ombudsman
17 Ombudsman offices across India covering all states
No court fee for Ombudsman complaints — free for policyholders
Awards binding on insurer if accepted by complainant; insurer cannot appeal to higher court
Online grievance portal: Bima Bharosa (IRDAI integrated grievance system)
Time limit: file Ombudsman complaint within 1 year of insurer's final rejection/no-response
consumer_rights: Free dispute resolution through Ombudsman without going to court
Right to ₹30 lakh compensation award (binding on insurer)
File complaint online through Bima Bharosa portal
Insurer cannot charge for Ombudsman proceedings

subject: Critical Illness and Cancer Insurance — Standardization of Definitions
key_points: Standard definitions for 37 critical illnesses published — all insurers must use same medical definitions
Cancer: minimum stage III for most cancers to trigger benefit (some earlier stages included with sub-limits)
Heart attack (myocardial infarction): ST-elevation MI confirmed by ECG and troponin test
Stroke: neurological deficit persisting over 24 hours, confirmed by imaging
Kidney failure: chronic irreversible failure requiring regular dialysis or transplant
Organ transplant: as recipient of liver, kidney, heart, lung transplant
Survival period: minimum 30 days after diagnosis to receive benefit (some insurers allow shorter)
Benefit: lump sum paid on diagnosis — not linked to treatment cost
consumer_rights: Right to standardized definitions — no ambiguity across insurers on what qualifies
Lump sum paid on diagnosis — no bills required
30-day survival clause must be disclosed clearly in policy document

subject: Telemedicine in Health Insurance — Coverage Mandate
key_points: Telemedicine consultations recognized as valid medical consultations for insurance claim purposes
Insurers must cover telemedicine OPD expenses where OPD is covered under the policy
Prescription issued via telemedicine is valid for claim submission
Video/audio consultation with a registered medical practitioner is sufficient
MCI (Medical Council of India) Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 recognized
Applies to all health policies with OPD benefit — cannot exclude telemedicine consultations
Important during COVID and for rural/remote patient access
consumer_rights: Telemedicine consultation costs are claimable where OPD is covered
Online prescriptions are valid for drug reimbursement claims
Right to use telemedicine without loss of insurance benefit

subject: Mental Health Coverage Mandate in Health Insurance
key_points: Mental Healthcare Act 2017 implementation: all health insurers must cover mental illness at par with physical illness
Psychiatric hospitalization must be covered like any other medical hospitalization
Cannot exclude mental illness as a standard exclusion — violates Mental Healthcare Act 2017
Substance abuse treatment can still be excluded (IRDAI allows this specific exclusion)
OPD psychiatry visits: covered where general OPD is covered
No separate waiting period for mental illness than for other illnesses
Effective implementation oversight by IRDAI ongoing since 2019
consumer_rights: Right to mental health hospitalization coverage equal to physical health
Cannot be denied health insurance purely on grounds of mental illness history
Mental illness OPD covered where other OPD is covered

subject: COVID-19 Insurance Coverage — Standard Products and Guidance
key_points: COVID-19 treatment covered under all standard health insurance policies (hospitalization expenses)
Corona Kavach: standard indemnity product specifically for COVID, SI ₹50,000 to ₹5 lakh, no waiting period for COVID
Corona Rakshak: benefit product, lump sum of ₹2.5L to ₹5L on positive COVID test requiring 72+ hours hospitalization
Home isolation with doctor recommendation covered under domiciliary benefit (where policy has this feature)
PPE kits, oximeters, pulse oximetry included as part of claim
Insurers cannot deny COVID claims citing standard exclusions like epidemic/pandemic
Group health policies must also cover COVID treatment
consumer_rights: Right to claim COVID hospitalization under existing health policies
Affordable COVID-specific standard products available
PPE and associated medical equipment cost is claimable

subject: Commission Disclosure — Agent and Broker Remuneration Transparency
key_points: Insurers must disclose commission/remuneration paid to agent/broker on policy document
Commission rates vary: term plans 10–15%, endowment 20–35% (first year), ULIP 5–15%
Agent commission for health insurance: 15–20% first year, 7.5–10% renewal year
Disclosure in policy schedule as mandatory — policyholder can see what intermediary earned
Corporate agents (banks) may earn higher commission — must be disclosed
Net Insurance Advisory (NIA) structure: fee-only basis, no commission
Conflict of interest disclosure required where agent is related to insurer
consumer_rights: Right to know commission paid to agent/broker from your premium
Commission disclosure helps identify potential mis-selling conflicts
Option to buy through fee-only advisors (NIAs) who charge flat fee, no commission

subject: e-Insurance Account — Digital Policy Repository
key_points: e-Insurance Account (eIA) enables holding all insurance policies in a single digital repository
Insurance repositories (NSDL, CDSL, CAMS, Karvy) authorized to maintain eIAs
All new insurance policies can be held in electronic format in eIA
KYC done once — valid for all policies in eIA (common KYC across policies)
Premium payment via eIA: single payment portal for all policies
Policy nomination and contact updates can be done centrally through eIA
Nominees can locate all policies through eIA upon policyholder death
Free to open and maintain eIA — no charges
consumer_rights: Right to hold all insurance policies digitally in one place
One-time KYC for all insurance policies
Nominees can find all policies through eIA repository

subject: Digital Insurance — Online Sales and Paperless Policies
key_points: All insurers must offer digital (online) policy issuance without requirement for physical signature
E-signature (Aadhaar OTP) or digital signature acceptable for policy documents
Policy documents must be issued digitally within 48 hours of premium payment confirmation
Mobile-based claims process: photos/videos as primary evidence for minor motor claims
Video KYC (VKYC) accepted for individual policy issuance — replaces in-person verification
WhatsApp and app-based claim intimation accepted by most insurers post this circular
Electronic policy documents carry same legal validity as physical policies
consumer_rights: Right to fully digital policy purchase without branch visit
E-signed policies have same legal validity as physical documents
Video KYC right — no need to visit insurer's branch for identity verification

subject: Insurance Repository — eIA Mandate and Implementation
key_points: Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India established insurance repositories to digitize insurance holdings
4 approved repositories: NSDL e-Governance, CDSL Insurance Repository, CAMS Insurance Repository, Karvy Insurance Repository
eIA (e-Insurance Account) is unique 10-digit number for each account holder
All policies converted to electronic format: physical policies surrendered and held electronically
Benefits: single view of all policies, easy updating of nominee/contact details, no policy loss risk
Integration with DigiLocker: insurance policies can be stored in DigiLocker linked to Aadhaar
consumer_rights: Free account at any repository of choice
Centralized view of all insurance policies across all insurers
Reduced risk of losing physical policy documents

subject: New-Age Health Products — Day Care, Chronic Disease Management, and Wellness
key_points: Insurers allowed to offer chronic disease management programs (diabetes, hypertension) integrated with health plans
Wellness benefit: premium discounts of up to 20% for maintaining health benchmarks (step count, BMI, regular checkups)
Day care procedures list expanded to 180+ — all must be covered
Mental health and addiction treatment can be bundled as riders
Telemedicine, digital health tools, wearable data integration allowed for wellness discounts
AI-based claim scrutiny for faster small claims (under ₹50,000) settlement — must be completed in 7 days
Network hospital quality standards — NABH accreditation preferred network hospitals get better rates
consumer_rights: Right to wellness discounts for healthy behavior
Comprehensive day care coverage for 180+ procedures
Faster AI-assisted claim settlement for small claims

subject: Bima Sugam — Unified Insurance Platform
key_points: Bima Sugam: proposed unified digital platform for all insurance transactions (buy, service, claim)
Single portal to compare and buy all types of insurance (life, health, motor, travel)
Integration with DigiLocker, ABHA (health account), Aadhaar, and UPI for seamless transactions
Policy-as-a-Service: policy can be purchased in under 5 minutes with Aadhaar eKYC
Claim intimation and tracking through Bima Sugam portal
Bima Vistar: affordable composite micro-insurance product for rural/uninsured population
Target: insuring every Indian family — Har Ghar Bima initiative
consumer_rights: Right to simplified comparison shopping across all insurers on one platform
Digital-first claim filing and tracking
Affordable Bima Vistar product for uninsured population

subject: Health Insurance — Specific Disease Waiting Period and PED Definition
key_points: PED (Pre-existing Disease) definition standardized: any condition for which medical advice, diagnosis, care, or treatment was received within 48 months before first policy inception
Named disease waiting periods capped: maximum 2 years for specific listed conditions (hernia, cataract, joint replacement, etc.)
Initial waiting period for all diseases: 30 days (except accidents, which have no waiting period)
Insurers cannot apply more than 4-year PED waiting period
Migration/porting: waiting period credit must be given based on continuous coverage
List of conditions that can have specific waiting period published — insurers cannot add conditions not in IRDAI list
Accidental injuries: covered from day 1 regardless of any waiting period
consumer_rights: PED clearly defined — 48-month lookback period
Accidents covered from day 1 — no waiting period
Maximum 2-year waiting period for specific listed conditions (not 4 years)

subject: Life Insurance — Benefit Illustration and Return on Investment Disclosure
key_points: Mandatory benefit illustrations at two guaranteed rates (4% and 8%) for traditional policies to show realistic return scenarios
ULIP illustrations: must show returns at 4% and 8% gross, net of all charges including FMC
IRR (Internal Rate of Return) must be disclosed for traditional policies alongside benefit illustration
Guaranteed vs non-guaranteed components must be clearly separated in all illustrations
Agent/bank is liable for mis-selling if benefit illustration shown was inflated or incorrect
Policy document must carry one page summarizing: premiums, guaranteed benefits, indicative returns, charges
ULIP: cost of insurance (mortality charge) must be separately disclosed annually
consumer_rights: Right to transparent benefit illustrations before purchase
IRR disclosure helps compare life insurance returns to other investments
Legal recourse for mis-selling if return projections were misrepresented

subject: Bima Bharosa — Integrated Grievance Redressal System
key_points: Bima Bharosa: IRDAI's integrated grievance management portal replacing old IGMS system
Single portal for all insurance grievances: life, health, motor, general insurance
First approach insurer's internal grievance cell (must resolve in 30 days); if unresolved, escalate to Bima Bharosa
Ombudsman can be approached directly through Bima Bharosa after 30 days of insurer non-resolution
Acknowledgment within 24 hours of grievance filing
Resolution timeline tracking: complainant can track status in real-time
IRDAI Bima Lokpal (Ombudsman) integrated with Bima Bharosa
Grievance escalation path: Insurer → Bima Bharosa/Ombudsman → Consumer Forum → Court
consumer_rights: Centralized grievance portal for all insurance complaints
24-hour acknowledgment guarantee
Real-time tracking of complaint resolution status
Free and easy access to Ombudsman through Bima Bharosa

subject: Master Circular on Health Insurance Products (2024) — Standardized Exclusions, Sub-Limits, and Portability Norms
key_points: Consolidates all prior health insurance product guidelines into a single master circular effective 1 April 2024

Standardized Exclusion List I: 16 permanent exclusions (cosmetic surgery, war/nuclear, adventure sports without rider, self-inflicted injury, substance abuse, obesity treatment, change-of-gender, sterility/infertility, unproven treatments, etc.)
Standardized Exclusion List II: 17 time-bound exclusions with 2-year specific disease waiting period (hernia, cataract, joint replacement, tonsillectomy, etc.)
Moratorium period reduced to 5 years for policies issued from 1 April 2024 — after 5 continuous years, insurer cannot contest claims citing non-disclosure except proven fraud
Sub-limits: insurers must disclose all sub-limits prominently on policy schedule front page — room rent, ICU, ambulance, cataract, maternity
Portability: policyholder can port within 45 days before renewal; new insurer must give full waiting period credit for continuous coverage
Day care procedures list expanded to 180+ mandatory coverages; insurers cannot exclude listed day care procedures
Restoration benefit: must restore sum insured at least once per policy year; restoration cannot be denied for same illness if used by different family member
consumer_rights: Right to transparent sub-limit disclosure on policy schedule front page
5-year moratorium protection — claims cannot be contested after 5 continuous years (except fraud)
Right to port health policy to any insurer without losing waiting period credit
Mandatory coverage of 180+ day care procedures across all health policies

subject: Policyholder Protection Regulations (2024) — Claim Settlement Timelines, Free Look, and Grievance Mechanism
key_points: Claim settlement: insurer must settle or reject claim within 30 days of receiving all required documents — mandatory for life, health, and general insurance
Interest on delayed settlement: if insurer fails to settle within 30 days, must pay interest at 2% above bank rate on the claim amount for the delay period
Free look period: 15 days for policies sold in person/branch; 30 days for policies sold through distance mode (online, telephone, email)
Grievance redressal: insurer must acknowledge complaint within 3 working days and resolve within 15 days (fast-track) or 30 days (standard)
Policy document issuance: must be issued within 30 days of acceptance of proposal; digital issuance within 48 hours where e-KYC is completed
Nomination: insurer must facilitate online nomination changes; beneficial nominee concept introduced — specific beneficiary identification
Unclaimed amounts: insurers must trace and pay unclaimed amounts proactively; annual disclosure of unclaimed amounts to IRDAI
Policyholder information: annual statement showing premiums paid, benefits accrued, surrender value, and policy status must be sent to every policyholder
consumer_rights: Right to claim settlement or rejection within 30 days with written reasons
Right to interest compensation on delayed claim settlements
Right to 15/30-day free look cancellation with full refund (minus stamp duty and proportionate risk premium)
Right to annual policy statement with complete benefit and value information

subject: Claim Settlement Guidelines — 30-Day Mandate, Interest on Delays, and Document Requirements
key_points: Cashless claims: insurer must respond to pre-authorization within 1 hour for planned hospitalization and 30 minutes for emergency
Final cashless settlement: within 3 hours of discharge or by end of same working day, whichever is later
Reimbursement claims: complete settlement within 30 days of receiving all documents; if additional documents needed, request within 7 days of claim intimation
Interest on delay: 2% above prevailing bank rate per annum for every day of delay beyond 30 days

Standard document list: claim form, hospital discharge summary, investigation reports, original bills, pharmacy bills, FIR (if accident) — insurer cannot demand documents beyond this standard list without specific justification
Rejection communication: written rejection with specific reasons, citing exact policy clause, within 30 days; oral rejection is not valid
Partial settlement: if part of claim is admissible, insurer must settle admissible portion within 30 days while disputing the balance
TPA responsibility: Third Party Administrator is agent of insurer — TPA delays are insurer's liability
consumer_rights: Right to cashless pre-authorization response within 1 hour (planned) or 30 minutes (emergency)
Right to written rejection with specific policy clause citation — oral rejection is invalid
Right to interest at 2% above bank rate for delays beyond 30 days
Right to partial settlement of admissible claim portion even while balance is disputed

subject: Insurance Ombudsman Rules 2017 (as Amended 2023) — Jurisdiction, Process, and Timelines
key_points: Jurisdiction: Ombudsman can hear complaints on policies with risk coverage up to ₹50 lakh (enhanced from ₹30 lakh by 2023 amendment)
Award limit: Ombudsman can award compensation up to ₹50 lakh (enhanced from ₹30 lakh by 2023 amendment)
17 Ombudsman offices across India; jurisdiction based on policyholder's residential address
Eligibility: complainant must first approach insurer's grievance cell; if no response in 30 days or unsatisfactory response, can approach Ombudsman
Time limit: complaint must be filed within 1 year of insurer's final response or 1 year and 30 days of complaint to insurer (whichever is later)
Process: Ombudsman first attempts mediation/conciliation; if unsuccessful, passes a binding award within 3 months of receiving complaint
Award is binding on insurer if accepted by complainant; insurer must comply within 30 days of award
No lawyer needed: process is informal, policyholder can represent themselves; no court fees
Online filing through Bima Bharosa portal (igms.irda.gov.in) or in person at Ombudsman office
consumer_rights: Free dispute resolution without lawyers or court fees
Award up to ₹50 lakh binding on insurer (2023 amendment)
3-month resolution timeline from Ombudsman
Online complaint filing through Bima Bharosa / IGMS portal

subject: ULIP Disclosure Regulations — Charge Transparency, NAV Reporting, and Benefit Illustration Standards
key_points: Fund Management Charge (FMC) capped at 1.35% per annum across all ULIP funds — no exceptions

All charges must be disclosed in a standardized charge schedule: premium allocation charge, policy administration charge, fund management charge, mortality charge, fund switching charge, discontinuance charge
NAV must be published daily on insurer's website by 10 PM; historical NAV data for past 5 years must be freely accessible
Benefit illustration mandatory at 4% and 8% gross return scenarios, shown NET of all charges including FMC
IRR (Internal Rate of Return) of the illustration must be disclosed alongside gross return assumption
Reduction in Yield (RiY): total impact of charges on returns must be expressed as percentage points per annum — e.g., 'charges reduce your return by 2.3% per year'
Discontinuance charge: maximum ₹6,000 or 2% of fund value (whichever is lower) in years 1-3; nil from year 4 onwards for regular premium ULIPs
Minimum 5-year lock-in: discontinued funds held in Discontinued Policy Fund earning minimum 4% per annum until completion of lock-in
consumer_rights: Right to daily NAV publication and 5-year NAV history
Right to benefit illustration showing returns NET of all charges
Right to clear disclosure of how charges reduce investment returns (Reduction in Yield)
Right to discontinuance fund earning minimum 4% during lock-in if policy is surrendered early

subject: Surrender Value Regulations (Revised 2024) — Enhanced GSV/SSV Norms for Traditional Life Policies
key_points: New Guaranteed Surrender Value (GSV) factors effective 1 October 2024 for all new traditional life policies
Year 2 surrender: minimum 30% of total premiums paid (up from ~20% under old norms)
Year 3 surrender: minimum 35% of total premiums paid
Year 5 surrender: minimum 50% of total premiums paid
Year 7 surrender: minimum 65% of total premiums paid
Year 10+: minimum 75-90% of total premiums paid depending on policy term
Special Surrender Value (SSV) = higher of GSV and (paid-up sum assured + accrued bonuses) discounted to present value
Insurer must pay the HIGHER of GSV and SSV — consumer always gets the better value
Annual statement must clearly show current GSV and SSV values
Single premium policies: minimum 90% of single premium as surrender value after 1 year
consumer_rights: Right to significantly higher guaranteed surrender values under 2024 norms
Right to the HIGHER of GSV and SSV — insurer cannot pay the lower amount
Right to clear annual communication of current surrender value (both GSV and SSV)
Protection against large value loss when exiting policies — minimum 50% return at year 5

subject: Mis-selling Prevention Guidelines — Definition, Red Flags, Remedies, and Insurer Accountability
key_points: Mis-selling defined: selling insurance product that does not meet the customer's stated needs, providing misleading information, or withholding material information

Specific mis-selling acts: guaranteeing returns on market-linked products, not disclosing charges, selling endowment/ULIP when customer needs term insurance, selling to ineligible persons (age/health), forging signatures on proposal forms
Insurer liability: insurer is liable for mis-selling by its agents, corporate agents, and distributors — cannot disclaim responsibility
Remedy: policyholder can file mis-selling complaint within 3 years of policy purchase; insurer must investigate and respond within 30 days
If mis-selling proved: full premium refund with interest at bank rate + compensation for financial loss suffered
Agent penalty: license cancellation, blacklisting from industry, financial penalty up to ₹1 crore on insurer for systemic mis-selling
Call recording: all telephonic sales calls must be recorded and stored for 3 years as evidence
Need analysis: mandatory need analysis document to be signed by customer before purchase of investment-cum-insurance products (ULIPs, endowments)
consumer_rights: Right to full premium refund with interest if mis-selling is proved
Right to file mis-selling complaint within 3 years of policy purchase
Right to access recorded sales call as evidence in dispute
Right to need analysis documentation before being sold investment-linked insurance products

subject: Insurance Advertisements and Disclosure Regulations 2021 — What Insurers Can and Cannot Claim
key_points: No insurance advertisement may use the word 'guaranteed' for non-guaranteed benefits or market-linked returns
Benefit illustrations in advertisements must show both 4% and 8% scenarios; cannot show only the higher scenario
Past performance disclaimers mandatory for ULIPs: 'Past performance does not guarantee future results'
Premium shown in ads must be for a clearly specified profile (age, SI, term) — no 'starting from ₹X/day' without full context
Claim settlement ratio can be advertised but must cite IRDAI source and financial year — cannot cherry-pick metrics
Comparative advertisements: cannot name competitor products; comparison must be fair and based on publicly available IRDAI data
Celebrity endorsements: celebrity must have actually purchased the product; disclosure of paid endorsement mandatory
Social media and digital ads: same rules apply as print/TV — IRDAI can penalize for misleading social media campaigns
Penalty: ₹25 lakh per violation for insurer; advertisement removal within 24 hours of IRDAI direction
consumer_rights: Right to truthful advertising — no misleading 'guaranteed return' claims on market-linked products
Right to see complete premium information (not just 'starting from' figures) in any advertisement
Right to complain to IRDAI about misleading insurance advertisements
Protection from misleading CSR/performance claims in insurer marketing

subject: Motor Insurance Guidelines — Third Party Premium, IDV Computation, and NCB Transfer Rules
key_points: Third Party (TP) premium rates fixed by IRDAI annually: private car ≤1000cc ₹2,094, 1000-1500cc ₹3,416, >1500cc ₹7,897; two-wheeler ≤75cc ₹538, 75-150cc ₹714, 150-350cc ₹1,366, >350cc ₹2,804

IDV (Insured Declared Value) = manufacturer's listed selling price minus IRDAI depreciation: <6 months 5%, 6-12 months 15%, 1-2 years 20%, 2-3 years 30%, 3-4 years 40%, 4-5 years 50%, >5 years by mutual agreement
NCB (No Claim Bonus) slabs: 20% after 1 claim-free year, 25% after 2, 35% after 3, 45% after 4, 50% after 5+ years — applies to OD premium only
NCB transfer: NCB belongs to the OWNER not the vehicle; can be transferred to new vehicle upon sale of old vehicle
Compulsory Personal Accident (CPA) cover: ₹15 lakh for owner-driver of private car and two-wheeler — mandatory
Own Damage (OD) and TP can be purchased from different insurers — standalone OD policy and standalone TP policy allowed
Long-term TP: 3-year TP for new private cars, 5-year TP for new two-wheelers — available at time of vehicle purchase
Salvage: insurer must offer policyholder option to retain salvage with agreed deduction in total loss claims
consumer_rights: Right to NCB belonging to owner — transferable to new vehicle upon sale
Right to purchase OD and TP from different insurers for competitive pricing
Right to retain salvage with deduction in total loss claims
Right to IRDAI-fixed TP premium rates — no insurer can charge more than scheduled rates

subject: Travel Insurance Guidelines — Minimum Coverage Standards, Standard Benefits, and Claim Procedures
key_points: Schengen visa requirement: minimum EUR 30,000 (approximately ₹27 lakh) medical coverage mandatory for all Schengen country travel

Standard coverage categories mandated: medical emergency and hospitalization, emergency medical evacuation, repatriation of remains, trip cancellation/interruption, baggage loss/delay, passport loss, personal liability, hijack distress allowance
Minimum coverage amounts for comprehensive travel plans: medical $50,000-500,000, trip cancellation $1,000-10,000, baggage loss $500-3,000, personal liability $50,000-200,000
Pre-existing condition coverage: most policies exclude PED; policies covering PED must clearly state terms (typically 50% copay, specific conditions only)
Adventure sports: excluded by default; optional rider available for trekking, skiing, scuba diving, bungee jumping at additional premium (20-50% extra)
Claim process: intimate insurer within 24 hours of incident; medical emergency claims processed through international TPA network; reimbursement within 30 days
Policy inception: coverage starts from departure (some policies from home to airport); ends on return to India
Student travel: extended policies up to 2 years for students with mental health coverage included from 2023
consumer_rights: Right to clearly stated coverage limits for all standard benefit categories
Right to 24-hour emergency assistance helpline (mandatory for all travel insurers)
Right to claim reimbursement within 30 days of document submission
Right to clear disclosure of PED exclusions and adventure sports limitations before purchase

subject: Group Insurance Regulations — Employer Group Health, Master Policyholder Rights, and Exit Coverage
key_points: Master policyholder (employer) must provide certificate of insurance to each group member with full coverage details
Group health policies must provide minimum ₹3 lakh sum insured per employee (IRDAI recommendation for employer groups >500 members)
Exit coverage: when employee leaves employer group, insurer must offer individual migration policy without fresh waiting periods — credit for all waiting periods served under group policy
Pre-existing disease coverage: group policies typically cover PED from day 1 with no waiting period (unlike individual policies)
Maternity coverage in group health: mandatory for groups >20 employees; typically covers normal delivery ₹25,000-50,000 and C-section ₹50,000-75,000
Dependents: spouse, dependent children (up to 25 years), and dependent parents can be covered under employer group policy
Claim process: TPA appointed by insurer handles claims; cashless network of insurer applies to group members
Renewal: insurer can increase premium based on claims experience but cannot refuse renewal mid-term or without 60-day notice
consumer_rights: Right to certificate of insurance with full coverage details from employer/master policyholder
Right to migrate to individual policy without losing waiting period credit upon leaving employment
Right to PED coverage from day 1 under most employer group health policies
Right to 60-day advance notice before renewal premium change or non-renewal

subject: Micro Insurance Regulations — Affordable Products for Low-Income Segments
key_points: Micro insurance products designed for economically vulnerable sections with simple terms and low premiums
Life micro insurance: sum insured ₹5,000 to ₹2,00,000; premium ₹100-1,000 per year; simple terms with minimal exclusions
General micro insurance: sum insured ₹5,000 to ₹1,00,000 for health/PA/property; premium ₹50-500 per year
Distribution: can be sold through NGOs, self-help groups, microfinance institutions, rural banks, and business correspondents
Simplified proposal form: minimal documentation required; no medical test for life micro up to ₹50,000 SI
Claim settlement: simplified process; claim must be settled within 10 days for micro products (faster than standard 30 days)
Bima Vistar: IRDAI's proposed composite micro-insurance product covering life, health, and property in single policy at ₹1-2/day
Government schemes: PMJJBY (₹436/year, ₹2L life cover) and PMSBY (₹20/year, ₹2L accident cover) as micro insurance benchmarks
consumer_rights: Right to affordable insurance products with simplified terms and minimal exclusions
Right to faster claim settlement (10 days) for micro insurance products
Right to purchase micro insurance through local institutions (NGOs, SHGs, cooperatives)
No medical test requirement for life micro insurance up to ₹50,000 sum insured

subject: Pre-Existing Disease (PED) Definition Circular — What Counts as PED, Moratorium, and Disclosure Requirements
key_points: PED defined: any condition, ailment, injury, or disease diagnosed or for which medical advice/treatment was received within 48 months prior to the date of first policy inception
48-month lookback: only conditions diagnosed/treated in the 48 months BEFORE first policy issuance qualify as PED — conditions before that window do not count
Moratorium period: after 8 continuous years of coverage (pre-2024 policies), insurer cannot reject claims citing non-disclosure of PED except in cases of proven fraud
2024 update: moratorium reduced to 5 years for policies issued from 1 April 2024
PED waiting period: maximum 4 years (48 months) from first policy inception — IRDAI cap applies universally
Portability credit: if policyholder has served 3 years PED waiting period with insurer A, insurer B must give credit for those 3 years upon portability
Disclosure obligation: policyholder must disclose all known PED in proposal form honestly; non-disclosure is different from misrepresentation
Insurer obligation: insurer should conduct pre-policy medical screening for high-risk applicants — cannot later claim non-disclosure if no medical was conducted
consumer_rights: Right to clear 48-month PED lookback — conditions before that window are not PED
Right to moratorium protection (8 years pre-2024, 5 years from 2024) — no PED rejection after continuous coverage
Right to PED waiting period credit upon portability to new insurer
Right to protection from PED rejection if insurer failed to conduct pre-policy medical screening

subject: Standard Product Guidelines — Arogya Sanjeevani (Health) and Saral Jeevan Bima (Life) Mandatory Products
key_points: Arogya Sanjeevani: mandatory standard health insurance product offered by all general and standalone health insurers from 1 April 2020
Arogya Sanjeevani features: SI ₹1 lakh to ₹5 lakh, 5% copay, no room rent sub-limit, AYUSH coverage, 30-day initial waiting, 48-month PED waiting, 24-month specific disease waiting
Product features identical across all insurers — only premium varies, enabling true comparison shopping
Saral Jeevan Bima: mandatory standard term life insurance product from all life insurers from 1 January 2021
Saral Jeevan Bima features: pure term plan, SI ₹5 lakh to ₹25 lakh, age 18-65 entry, term 5-40 years, minimal exclusions (only suicide in first year)
Saral Jeevan Bima: no policy administration charge, no medical test up to ₹5 lakh SI for age below 45
Standard Personal Accident (Saral Suraksha Bima): mandatory PA product from 1 April 2021, SI ₹1 lakh to ₹1 crore
Purpose: ensure every Indian has access to basic, understandable insurance products at competitive prices
consumer_rights: Right to buy Arogya Sanjeevani from any non-life/health insurer — cannot be refused
Right to buy Saral Jeevan Bima (standard term) from any life insurer — cannot be refused
True comparison shopping: identical product features across insurers, only premium differs
Simple, transparent products with no hidden sub-limits or complex terms

subject: Insurance Surveyor and Loss Assessor Regulations — Motor Claims Survey Process and Timelines
key_points: Surveyor must be appointed within 72 hours of claim intimation for motor own-damage claims
Survey must be completed within 48 hours of surveyor appointment (total 5 days from intimation to survey completion)
Surveyor must provide detailed assessment report including: damage extent, repair vs replacement recommendation, fair market cost of repairs, depreciation applied per IRDAI schedule
Re-inspection: policyholder can request second survey if dissatisfied with first assessment; insurer must appoint independent surveyor
Spot survey for minor claims (under ₹50,000): can be done through photographs and video — no physical surveyor visit needed
Cashless motor repair: surveyor authorizes repair at network garage; insurer settles directly with garage
Total loss: if repair cost exceeds 75% of IDV, vehicle is declared total loss; insurer pays IDV minus salvage value
Surveyor independence: surveyor is licensed by IRDAI and must not have financial interest in insurer or garage
consumer_rights: Right to surveyor appointment within 72 hours of claim intimation
Right to request re-inspection by independent surveyor if dissatisfied
Right to photo/video-based spot survey for minor claims without waiting for physical survey
Right to choose between repair and total loss settlement when repair cost exceeds 75% of IDV

subject: KYC and Anti-Money Laundering (AML) Guidelines for Insurance — Verification Requirements
key_points: KYC mandatory for all insurance policies: identity proof (Aadhaar, PAN, Passport, Voter ID) and address proof required
Video KYC (VKYC): permitted for individual policy issuance — live video verification with geo-tagging replaces in-person KYC
Aadhaar e-KYC: Aadhaar OTP-based verification accepted for instant policy issuance (with UIDAI consent)
PAN mandatory for: single premium >₹50,000, annual premium >₹50,000, any life policy with SA >₹5 lakh
Simplified KYC for micro insurance: self-certification with any one government ID sufficient for policies up to ₹50,000 SI
Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD): required for high-value policies (premium >₹10 lakh/year), politically exposed persons, and high-risk customers
Suspicious Transaction Reports (STR): insurers must file STR with Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) for unusual premium payments or policy transactions
Beneficial ownership: for corporate/trust policies, ultimate beneficial owner must be identified and verified
consumer_rights: Right to Video KYC — no mandatory branch visit for identity verification
Right to Aadhaar e-KYC for instant policy issuance without physical document submission
Right to simplified KYC for micro insurance products (single government ID sufficient)
Right to privacy — insurer can collect only KYC data necessary for insurance, not extraneous personal data

subject: Digital Insurance Regulations — E-Policy Issuance, Electronic Records, and Digital-First Processes
key_points: E-policy has same legal validity as physical policy under IT Act 2000 and Indian Evidence Act amendments
All insurers must offer digital policy issuance option — cannot force physical-only issuance
Policy document must be issued electronically within 48 hours of premium payment confirmation
E-signature (Aadhaar OTP or DSC) acceptable for proposal form, policy document, and endorsements
Digital claims: WhatsApp, app-based, email claim intimation accepted; insurer cannot insist on physical forms
Electronic premium payment: UPI, net banking, auto-debit mandate, digital wallets all recognized
Policy storage: insurers must provide online portal/app for policyholders to access all policy documents digitally
Consent framework: digital consent (OTP-based or click-to-accept) valid for policy purchase, with full terms displayed before consent
consumer_rights: Right to fully digital policy purchase, service, and claims without branch visit
Right to electronic policy document within 48 hours of payment
Right to digital claim intimation through WhatsApp, app, or email
Right to access all policy documents online through insurer portal/app at any time

subject: Mental Healthcare Act 2017 Compliance — Mandatory Mental Health Coverage in Insurance
key_points: Section 21(4) of Mental Healthcare Act 2017: every insurer must make provision for medical insurance for treatment of mental illness on the same basis as for physical illness
Mental illness hospitalization must be covered identically to physical illness hospitalization — same sub-limits, same copay, same room category
Cannot exclude mental illness as a standard permanent exclusion — such exclusion violates the Mental Healthcare Act and is void
Psychiatric hospitalization in recognized mental health establishments covered — both government and private facilities
OPD psychiatric consultations: covered where policy covers general OPD — cannot selectively exclude mental health OPD
No separate or additional waiting period for mental illness beyond what applies to other conditions
Substance abuse treatment: IRDAI allows exclusion of substance abuse/addiction treatment as a separate exclusion (not linked to mental health exclusion)
Insurer cannot charge higher premium, refuse to issue policy, or apply discriminatory terms solely based on mental illness history
consumer_rights: Right to mental health hospitalization coverage at par with physical health — no discrimination
Right to not be denied insurance or charged higher premium solely due to mental illness history
Right to psychiatric OPD coverage where general OPD is covered in the policy
Mental illness exclusion clause in any health policy is legally void under Mental Healthcare Act 2017

subject: Senior Citizen Health Insurance Guidelines — Mandatory Coverage, Pricing Norms, and Renewal Guarantees
key_points: Insurers must offer at least one health insurance product for entry age 65+ (many insurers now offer entry up to 80 years)
Lifelong renewal guarantee: once issued, insurer cannot refuse renewal at any age — guaranteed renewal for life
Premium increase at renewal: must be actuarially justified and filed with IRDAI; cannot increase premium beyond 25% in any single renewal year without IRDAI approval
Copay for senior citizens: maximum 20% copay allowed for individual senior citizen policies (some insurers charge 10-20% above age 60)
PED coverage: standard 48-month PED waiting applies; insurers encouraged to offer reduced PED waiting (24 months) for senior products
Government subsidy: under Ayushman Bharat PM-JAY, senior citizens from eligible families get ₹5 lakh free health cover per family per year
Section 80D tax benefit: senior citizens get enhanced deduction of ₹50,000 (vs ₹25,000 for non-seniors) for health insurance premium
Day care and domiciliary treatment: particularly important for seniors — must be covered without additional exclusions
consumer_rights: Right to lifelong guaranteed renewal — insurer cannot refuse renewal based on age or claims history
Right to actuarially justified premium increases — no arbitrary senior citizen premium hikes
Right to buy health insurance up to age 65+ from at least one product per insurer
Enhanced ₹50,000 tax deduction under Section 80D for senior citizen health insurance premium

subject: Renewable Policy Guidelines — Guaranteed Renewal Rights, Grace Period, and Non-Renewal Protections
key_points: All individual health insurance policies (indemnity type) must be offered with lifelong renewal guarantee from 2022
Grace period: minimum 30 days from premium due date for renewal payment without loss of coverage; policy remains active during grace period
Claims during grace period: if hospitalization occurs during 30-day grace period and premium is paid subsequently, claim is valid
Non-renewal by insurer: insurer can only non-renew for fraud or misrepresentation — cannot non-renew based on claims experience or age
Premium revision at renewal: insurer can revise premium only with IRDAI-approved revised rate tables — individual risk-based repricing not allowed
Continuity benefit: all waiting periods, PED credit, and no-claim bonuses carry forward on renewal without interruption
Lapsed policy revival: if policy lapses (premium not paid within grace period), can be revived within 15-30 days with underwriting; waiting periods restart for revival after 30 days
Migration within insurer: policyholder can migrate to a higher/different plan from same insurer at renewal with waiting period credit
consumer_rights: Right to lifelong guaranteed renewal of individual health policies
Right to 30-day grace period for renewal payment without loss of coverage
Right to valid claim if hospitalized during grace period and premium subsequently paid
Right to not be non-renewed based on claims history or age — only fraud-based non-renewal allowed

subject: Women-Specific Insurance Product Guidelines — Maternity, Critical Illness, and Financial Independence
key_points: Insurers encouraged to design women-centric health products covering: maternity (normal + C-section + complications), women-specific cancers (breast, cervical, ovarian), osteoporosis, PCOS, thyroid disorders
Maternity coverage: mandatory in all comprehensive health plans with SI above ₹10 lakh (sub-limits apply: normal delivery ₹25,000-50,000, C-section ₹50,000-75,000, new-born cover 90 days)
Women-specific critical illness riders: must cover breast cancer, cervical cancer, ovarian cancer, and complications of pregnancy at defined benefit amounts
Premium discount for women: insurers may offer 5-10% premium discount for female policyholders in term and health insurance (reflecting lower mortality/morbidity)
Working women's group insurance: employer must provide maternity leave coverage and health benefits for women employees as per Maternity Benefit Act 2017
Single women / widows / divorcees: cannot be charged higher premium or denied coverage based on marital status
IRDAI directive: at least one product per insurer should be designed specifically addressing women's health and financial protection needs
consumer_rights: Right to maternity coverage in comprehensive health plans above ₹10 lakh SI
Right to not be discriminated in premium or coverage based on gender or marital status
Right to women-specific critical illness coverage including breast and cervical cancer
Right to new-born baby coverage for 90 days from birth under mother's health policy

subject: Section 45 — Contestability Period Guidelines for Life Insurance Policies
key_points: Section 45 of Insurance Act 1938 (as amended 2015): no life insurance policy can be called into question after 3 years from date of policy issuance
Within 3 years (contestability period): insurer can investigate and reject claim for misrepresentation, fraud, or non-disclosure in proposal form
Burden of proof on insurer: insurer must prove that (a) statement was material, (b) statement was inaccurate/false, and (c) policyholder knew it was inaccurate at time of declaration
After 3 years: policy is incontestable — insurer cannot reject death claim or maturity claim citing non-disclosure, even if non-disclosure is discovered
Exception: fraud with intent to deceive can be contested even after 3 years, but burden of proof remains on insurer and is extremely high
Pre-2015 policies: 2-year contestability period applied (Section 45 amended in 2015 to extend to 3 years)
Practical impact: if policyholder dies in year 4 and insurer discovers undisclosed diabetes, claim MUST be paid — policy is incontestable
Distinction from health moratorium: health insurance has 8-year (now 5-year) moratorium under IRDAI circular; life insurance has statutory 3-year contestability under Section 45
consumer_rights: Right to incontestable life policy after 3 years — insurer cannot reject claims citing non-disclosure
Burden of proof always on insurer during contestability period — policyholder presumed honest
Death claim must be paid after 3 years regardless of any non-disclosure discovered later (except proven fraud)
Clear statutory protection under Section 45 of Insurance Act — overrides any policy terms to the contrary

subject: Insurance Broker Regulations — Licensing, Duties, Disclosure, and Client Protection
key_points: Insurance broker: licensed intermediary who can sell products from multiple insurers (unlike agent who represents one insurer)
Broker categories: direct broker (works with policyholder), reinsurance broker, composite broker (both direct and reinsurance)
Fiduciary duty: broker must act in the best interest of the client, not the insurer — unlike agent who represents insurer
Disclosure: broker must disclose commission/remuneration received from insurer to the client before policy issuance
Needs analysis: broker must conduct documented needs analysis to recommend suitable products — liability for mis-selling
Claim assistance: broker must assist client in claim filing, documentation, and follow-up with insurer as part of service obligation
Minimum capital: ₹75 lakh for direct broker, ₹2 crore for reinsurance broker, ₹2.5 crore for composite broker
E&O insurance: brokers must maintain Errors & Omissions professional liability insurance to compensate clients for broker negligence
IRDAI web aggregator: online platforms that compare insurance products must be licensed as web aggregators; comparison must be fair and unbiased
consumer_rights: Right to broker's fiduciary duty — broker must act in your interest, not insurer's
Right to commission disclosure — know what your broker earns from recommending a product
Right to claim assistance from broker throughout the claims process
Right to compensation through broker's E&O insurance if broker's negligence causes financial loss

subject: Reinsurance Guidelines — Consumer Impact of Reinsurance Arrangements on Claim Settlement
key_points: Reinsurance is insurance for insurers — primary insurer transfers part of risk to reinsurer; does NOT affect policyholder's claim rights
Policyholder's claim is ALWAYS against the primary insurer — reinsurer has no direct obligation to policyholder
Primary insurer cannot delay or deny claim citing pending reinsurance settlement — must pay from own funds first
GIC Re (General Insurance Corporation of India): India's sole national reinsurer; provides mandatory cession of 5% of all general insurance premiums
Solvency ratio: reinsurance helps insurers maintain required 1.5x solvency margin — protects policyholder against insurer insolvency
Catastrophe coverage: reinsurance ensures insurers can pay large-scale claims (floods, earthquakes, pandemics) without going bankrupt
IRDAI oversight: reinsurance arrangements are approved by IRDAI; cross-border reinsurance with foreign reinsurers requires IRDAI clearance
Policyholder protection fund: in event of insurer insolvency, policyholder protection fund (maintained by IRDAI) provides safety net up to defined limits
consumer_rights: Right to claim payment from primary insurer regardless of reinsurance arrangement status
Right to insurer solvency protection through mandatory reinsurance requirements
Right to policyholder protection fund coverage in event of insurer insolvency
Insurer cannot cite reinsurance delays as reason to delay your claim settlement

subject: Anti-Fraud Framework for Insurance — Detection, Prevention, and Policyholder Protection
key_points: Every insurer must establish an anti-fraud policy with dedicated fraud investigation unit
Types of insurance fraud: policyholder fraud (inflated claims, staged accidents), intermediary fraud (forgery, premium misappropriation), insurer fraud (claim rejection without basis)
Fraud investigation timeline: insurer must complete investigation and communicate findings within 45 days of suspecting fraud — cannot indefinitely hold claims
If fraud NOT proved within 45 days: insurer must settle the claim and can pursue separate legal action later
Policyholder protection: legitimate claim cannot be delayed beyond 45 days on suspicion of fraud — anti-fraud process cannot be used as claim delay tactic
Data analytics: IRDAI mandates insurers use data analytics and AI for fraud detection while protecting genuine claimants
IIB (Insurance Information Bureau): central fraud database maintained by IIB for cross-insurer fraud detection
Whistleblower protection: insurers must have mechanism for employees and policyholders to report fraud anonymously
Penalty for fraudulent claims: policy voidance, claim forfeiture, criminal prosecution under IPC Sections 420/468
consumer_rights: Right to claim settlement within 45 days even if fraud investigation is underway (unless fraud is proven)
Right to not have legitimate claims delayed under guise of fraud investigation
Right to report insurance fraud anonymously through whistleblower mechanism
Right to due process — insurer must prove fraud; burden of proof is on insurer, not policyholder

subject: Telemedicine and OPD Coverage Guidelines — Digital Consultation, E-Pharmacy, and Outpatient Benefits
key_points: Telemedicine consultations via registered medical practitioners are valid for all insurance purposes: claim filing, prescription, referral, and pre-authorization
OPD (Outpatient Department) coverage: insurers encouraged to offer OPD benefit covering consultations, diagnostics, pharmacy, and physiotherapy up to defined annual limits
E-pharmacy: medicines purchased from licensed e-pharmacies (1mg, PharmEasy, Netmeds, Apollo Pharmacy online) with valid prescription are reimbursable
Annual health check-up: policies with OPD benefit must cover at least one annual health check-up — preventive health as part of insurance
Teleconsultation platforms: insurers may partner with telemedicine platforms to provide free/subsidized consultations as wellness benefit
OPD claim process: simplified claim — submit prescription, bills, and consultation record via app; settlement within 7 days for OPD claims under ₹5,000
Mental health teleconsultation: psychiatric and psychological counseling via telemedicine must be covered at par with in-person consultation
Second medical opinion via telemedicine: covered as part of claim process — policyholder can get second opinion before surgery/major treatment
consumer_rights: Right to use telemedicine consultations without loss of insurance coverage or claim validity
Right to e-pharmacy reimbursement with valid prescription from licensed online pharmacies
Right to mental health teleconsultation coverage at par with in-person psychiatric visits
Right to second medical opinion via telemedicine as part of claim evaluation process

subject: Insurance Product Withdrawal Guidelines — Policyholder Protection During Product Discontinuation
key_points: Insurer cannot withdraw/discontinue a product without 90-day prior intimation to IRDAI and existing policyholders
Existing policyholders' rights: withdrawal of product does NOT affect existing policies — all policies continue until maturity/expiry on same terms
Renewal guarantee: even if product is withdrawn from sale, existing policyholders' renewal rights are protected — insurer must continue renewing
Migration option: insurer must offer migration to a comparable or superior product at renewal if original product is withdrawn
ULIP fund closure: if ULIP fund is being merged/closed, policyholder must be given 30 days to switch to another fund without charge
Premium rates: existing premium guarantee (if any) is honored even after product withdrawal — insurer cannot revise premium for withdrawn product
Group policies: if group product is withdrawn, master policyholder must be informed 90 days before renewal with migration alternatives
IRDAI approval: product withdrawal must be approved by IRDAI; IRDAI can refuse withdrawal if it harms policyholder interests
consumer_rights: Right to continued policy terms even after product is withdrawn from market
Right to guaranteed renewal of withdrawn product on same terms
Right to migrate to comparable or superior product at no extra cost upon product withdrawal
Right to 90-day advance notice before any product withdrawal or discontinuation

subject: Data Protection and Privacy in Insurance — Policyholder Data Rights and Insurer Obligations
key_points: Policyholder data (health records, financial data, personal details) is confidential — insurer must maintain strict data protection protocols
Consent-based collection: insurer can collect only data necessary for underwriting, claims, and servicing — cannot collect extraneous personal data without explicit consent
Data sharing: policyholder health data cannot be shared with third parties (employers, other insurers, marketers) without explicit written consent
DPDP Act 2023 alignment: insurers must comply with Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 — data principal (policyholder) rights of access, correction, and erasure apply
Data retention: insurer must retain policyholder records for minimum 8 years after policy expiry; after retention period, data must be securely deleted/anonymized
Breach notification: insurer must notify IRDAI and affected policyholders within 72 hours of any data breach affecting personal data
Cross-border data transfer: policyholder data cannot be transferred outside India except to IRDAI-approved jurisdictions with adequate data protection
Data portability: policyholder has right to receive their complete policy and claims data in machine-readable format for portability to another insurer
consumer_rights: Right to data privacy — health records and personal data cannot be shared without explicit consent
Right to data access, correction, and erasure under DPDP Act 2023
Right to 72-hour breach notification if personal insurance data is compromised
Right to data portability — receive complete policy and claims data in portable format

subject: Insurance for Persons with Disabilities — Non-Discrimination, Accessibility, and Reasonable Accommodation
key_points: Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016 (RPwD Act): insurers cannot deny insurance to persons with disabilities on grounds of disability alone
Underwriting: disability-based loading or exclusion must be actuarially justified and specific to the disability's impact on insured risk — blanket rejection prohibited
Physical disability and health insurance: persons with physical disability (locomotor, visual, hearing) cannot be denied health insurance — physical disability is not a pre-existing disease
Mental disability: covered under Mental Healthcare Act 2017 for health insurance; cannot be excluded (see circ_038)
Accessible formats: policy documents must be available in accessible formats — large print, Braille, audio, and screen-reader-compatible digital format upon request
Online accessibility: insurer websites and apps must meet WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards for visually impaired users
Premium loading: if disability-based premium loading is applied, insurer must provide written actuarial justification; policyholder can challenge with IRDAI
Claim process: insurer must provide reasonable accommodation in claim process for persons with disabilities — home visits for survey, accessible communication
consumer_rights: Right to not be denied insurance solely on grounds of disability — blanket rejection is illegal
Right to policy documents in accessible formats (Braille, audio, large print, screen-reader compatible)
Right to challenge disability-based premium loading with IRDAI if actuarial justification is inadequate
Right to reasonable accommodation in claim filing and settlement process

subject: E-Insurance Account (eIA) Regulations — Digital Policy Repository, Operations, and Consumer Benefits
key_points: eIA (e-Insurance Account): unique 10-digit electronic account to hold all insurance policies in dematerialized form
Four approved Insurance Repositories: NSDL Insurance Repository (formerly SHCIL), CDSL Insurance Repository, CAMS Insurance Repository, KFin Technologies Insurance Repository
Free to open and maintain — no annual charges; policyholder can open eIA with any one repository
Single KYC: KYC done once with repository is valid for all policies — no need for separate KYC for each insurer
Centralized view: all life, health, motor, and general insurance policies visible in one dashboard
Operations through eIA: nomination changes, contact updates, premium payments, and policy servicing can be done centrally
Nominee benefit: in event of policyholder's death, nominee/legal heir can access all policies through eIA — prevents policy loss/unknown policy problem
DigiLocker integration: insurance policies in eIA can be linked to Aadhaar-based DigiLocker for additional digital access
Mandatory conversion: IRDAI encourages all existing physical policies to be converted to electronic form through eIA
eIA is portable — policyholder can switch repository while retaining all policy records
consumer_rights: Right to free eIA account with any approved Insurance Repository
Right to hold all insurance policies digitally in one consolidated view
Right to single KYC valid across all insurers through eIA repository
Nominee's right to discover all policyholder's policies through eIA in event of death
